data.title: Eni Returned Over 100% Since My Buy Call: Here Is How Much Upside Is Left
data.description:
Introduction to Eni

Chances are you traveled during Easter, and if you did so by car, you've felt the pain every time you had to fill up your car at the gas pump. Part of the relief might have come from your gains in case you were invested in oil stocks. But the news of the ceasefire in Iran is even more welcome, as we hope the war comes soon to an end.

On this news, oil stocks are down several percentage points, and I want to use this drop to revisit one of my best calls in this space: E Eni S.p.A. (E), which I considered a buy in October 2024 and has since then returned over 100% (S&P 500 (SP500): +16%). At that time, I thought that Eni was particularly interesting, as it was developing from a pure upstream player into a company with key satellite businesses, such as Enilive (biofuels) and Plenitude (renewables), both of which were attracting funds at valuations that basically implied that Eni's stock was trading at a big discount if we used a sum-of-the-parts valuation model (basically, its upstream business was priced at 3 EV/EBIT).

The repricing happened and, pay attention, it was not entirely triggered by the recent war in Iran. It started much earlier as confirmation signals from the satellite business made investors realize that Eni was trading at a discount, as we see below. Indeed, Eni more than doubled its price returns (not counting the generous yield, which was almost 8% less than two years ago and which still is close to 6% if we consider its buybacks).

Eni might not be familiar to some of my readers, especially those who don't live in Europe. It is one of the largest European oil companies, and it is headquartered in Rome (the Italian government has a 30.5% stake). Its upstream business represents the core of the company, generating around 90% of the company's adj. EBIT. Eni's portfolio is exposed to key African areas (Congo, Angola, Libya, Egypt, Cote d'Ivoire, Mozambique), as well as Kazakhstan and Indonesia.

Eni has a high replacement ratio of 167% (for every barrel produced, Eni replaces it with 1.67 barrels in proved reserves). This is a key metric for an oil company because it tells us that its resources are not depleting. Eni is also a key player in gas and LNG, being one of the largest European suppliers. Eni reports a global gas and LNG portfolio, but this doesn't include exploration and production; it is the trading and offtake business. Eni played a key role in replacing Russian gas with other suppliers.

The Satellite-Model

But Eni is developing a satellite business model, which is becoming cash-generative, with EUR 16B expected to come in from 2026 through 2030. The two main legs of this unit are Enilive and Plenitude, the former being a biofuel producer business, and the latter being a renewables and retail energy business. There is also the refining and chemicals business, which currently operates at a loss. This is why Eni is gradually repurposing its refineries from traditional operations to biorefineries.

Let's take a closer look at this part of the company.

In 2025, Eni Ares Management Alternative Credit funds, affiliates of Ares Management Corporation (ARES), signed an agreement for a 20% stake in Plenitude valued at around EURB (implying an equity valuation of EUR 10B, and an EV above EUR 12B).

Considering that the fund KKR acquired a 30% stake in Enilive at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 12, at the end of FY25 Eni reported over EUR 23B of EV highlighted by these satellite transactions.

Plenitude is not done. It has signed an agreement with Acea Energia, which should add 1.4M retail customers to its portfolio, so that Plenitude would reach its 11M goal originally set for 2028 two years ahead of schedule. There is a risk of regulator approval, but in a few months, we should know the final verdict.

Finally, the satellite model is growing because a carbon capture and storage division is growing. It is still in an early stage, but it has already attracted the capital of Global Infrastructure Partners (part of BlackRock), which holds a 49.99% stake in this business unit.

The pattern is clear. Eni identifies businesses with a high transition value, so that it can sign agreements with institutional investors that help validate the valuation. Eni holds control of the unit and generates cash flows that help it strengthen its balance sheet and fund its upstream growth.

Eni recently hit the news after it signed a €500M loan with the European Investment Bank to convert part of its Sannazzaro de' Burgondi's refinery (a facility doing 180,000 bbl/d) into a biorefinery, which further incentivizes me to update my sum-of-the-parts valuation and my general overview of this oil company that plays a key role in the Mediterranean and Africa. The production target for the biorefinery is 550,000 t/year of HVO diesel and SAF, starting in 2028. This is the second EIB loan in a year, after the one for the conversion of the Livorno refinery. Eni surely deserves institutional confidence, and this is why we have to understand that receiving an EIB loan has key advantages. In fact, these loans have rates that reflect EU sovereign credit instead of Eni's cost of capital. As a result, there is a clear advantage compared to corporate bond funding: rates are lower, and this allows the return rates to be higher. Moreover, this loan ties the debt to the conversion project, which means that the balance sheet of the company is protected.

Eni's Financials

If we look at Eni's FY25 results, it seems as if we are looking at a company reporting from ages ago: Brent averaged $69/bbl (FY24: $80/bbl). However, the stock held well and even moved up as the company showed operational resilience. Even though the adj. EBIT declined by 15% to EUR 12.22B; the two largest satellites, Enilive and Plenitude, reported a combined 6% EBIT growth to EUR 1.21B, representing now 10% of the company's overall EBIT. The adj. CFFO was down 8%, but was still strong: EUR 12.5B; capex remained under control, declining by 3 pps to EUR 8.5B. In the meantime, however, production increased by 7%, which was a strong signal: the business is growing while its costs are well managed. Installed renewable capacity increased by 41% YoY to 5.8GW.

All of this contributed to continued balance sheet improvements, which are shown below. The company always keeps around EUR 15B at hand, while its total debt decreased by EUR 2.5B YoY to EUR 34.2B, which means that its net debt/EBITDA ratio is around 1.5x. Keep in mind that, while deleveraging the balance sheet, Eni raised its dividends and increased its buybacks. Secondly, Eni lowered its gearing (the net debt/total capital ratio) to 14%, which means that only 14 cents of every euro Eni employs is financed, while the remaining 85 cents is equity.

For oil majors, gearing is extremely important because earnings are volatile (we've just seen a clear example of this, as oil prices soared and are now moving back down sharply). If gearing is too high, when the cycle moves south, the company could be in a dangerous position. Low gearing, on the other hand, allows a company to have the flexibility to invest throughout every phase of the cycle, while staying committed to the dividend and being able to face any negative shocks without too much fear.

In an environment where oil prices (CO1:COM) are volatile and are still close to $100 per barrel, it can be easy to think that Eni's distributions will likely increase this year, with an extra dividend and extra buyback that could be announced down the road on top of the EUR 1.5B buyback announced in mid-March. For sure, compared to the past, the company has now raised its ambitions and wants to return between 35% and 45% of its operating cash compared to the previous target (35%-40%), with an extra dividend being paid if Brent prices, on average, stay above $90.

Eni's Valuation

Is Eni a deal right now? When I say this, I am not going to argue whether the stock will drop or will rise in the next few months. Most of this will depend on what happens between the U.S. and Iran. But I want to see whether the case I built a year and a half ago still holds. Eni retains 70% of Enilive and 65% of Plenitude, which means that the combined enterprise value given the deals with KKR and ARES is around EUR 16.2B. We can then consider its gas and power business unit, whose EBIT is around EUR 1.4B. Using a multiple of 8, we have an estimated value of EUR 11.1B. Refining and chemicals are basically zero, while the nascent carbon business, where GIP has a stake, could be valued around EUR 500M.

We can now consider Eni's core business: exploration and production. Considering that it brings in around 11.2B in adj EBIT and its EBITDA could be around EUR 15.7B (I estimate 4.5B in D&A), with a 4.5 multiple, we have an EV of EUR 70.4B. The total is close to EUR 100B. We can then subtract the net debt of EUR 19B, and we have an equity value of EUR 81B. The shares outstanding are 2.9B, so the fair price is EUR 27.9. At the time of writing, the stock trades at EUR 23.50 in Milan, which means that the upside has thinned, but a nice 19% is still there. The ADR is current $55.10, but the target price in EUR equals to $63.1 (at EUR/USD 1.13; each ADR represents 2 shares), which reduces the upside to 14.5%.

There is, however, one catalyst that could materialize: Plenitude's IPO. Deconsolidation has already been announced, and if an IPO follows, we could see renewed interest among investors to have a stake in Eni.

I think we are close to fair value territory because the margin of safety here is thinner. The valuation could change if Brent prices stay above $100. But in the base scenario where the war ends and oil prices gradually normalize, I think Eni is now close to its intrinsic value and, therefore, I rate it as a Hold.

data.title: BrandywineGLOBAL - High Yield Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The U.S. fixed income market edged higher during the fourth quarter. The market was supported by central bank rate cuts, resilient economic growth, and overall solid investor demand. These factors more than offset tariff policy, uncertainty, concerns about higher unemployment, and a 43-day government shutdown in the U.S. As expected, the Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its benchmark interest rate 0.25% (25 basis points) at its meetings in October and December. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose three basis points (bps), ending the quarter at 4.18%. Falling US yields on the front end and continued strong demand were supportive of the credit market and kept spreads in a narrow range despite credit making headlines after Tricolor and First Brands quickly filed for bankruptcy. U.S. investment-grade and high-yield spreads marginally widened over the quarter. Primary market activity in the fourth quarter finished strong, capping the strongest year of issuance for both investment-grade and high-yield markets since the low rate environment of 2020-2021.
  • Contributors: Issue selection in the basic industry sector.
  • Detractors: Issue selection overall.
  • Outlook: Positive economic growth, business-friendly tax policy, and an accommodative Federal Reserve should benefit the corporate world.

Performance Review

  • Negative credit selection detracted from returns over the quarter. While the tariff-induced flight to the largest and highest rated issuers, regardless of valuation, has abated, in our view, credit events for Tricolor and First Brands late in the third quarter and subsequent “credit cockroach” comments from JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon elicited an initial sell off, and a broader risk-off sentiment among the lower quality cohorts of the market. This led to spread widening for credits rated below BB over the quarter, with credits rated CCC and below posting a negative return.
  • Idiosyncratic risks in a handful of our retail exposures were the leading detractors. These select issuers have undergone, or are undergoing, debt restructurings to address their respective challenges, and have been downgraded by rating agencies as a result. Our largest portfolio detractor for the quarter skipped an interest payment due in December, with a potential bankruptcy looming in 2026.
  • Selection in basic industry contributed positively, as detractors from the first half of the year continued to improve, boosted by a stronger commodities backdrop.
  • A moderate underweight duration position relative to the index slightly detracted over the period.

Outlook

  • High-yield has been on a positive run since the end of 2022, supported by the strength of equities on one side and benefiting from lower yields, much like core fixed income, on the other. While spread and yield have compressed over this period, the current environment remains supportive.
  • Positive economic growth, business-friendly tax policy, and an accommodative Fed should benefit the corporate world, while starting yields between 6% and 7% are attractive relative to the last decade.
  • Since June 2023, high-yield spreads have been in a range of 260 to 460 basis points. Defaults have been less than half the historic average and recoveries have been higher than average, while overall credit quality has improved. Absent a sustained bear market in equities or core fixed income, we expect this spread range to hold.
  • However, spreads closer to the middle of the range may be required as new issuance continues to flood the market. The flight to the largest, highest rated issuers has abated somewhat from the peak of the tariff-induced volatility. We expect that to continue moving forward. We have also worked through much of the idiosyncratic risks and reduced exposure to smaller, lower quality credits to mitigate our downside risk.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Bulls Are Back In Vengeance After The U.S.-Iran Ceasefire
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US-Iran ceasefire is now official, and stock market bulls are back in vengeance.

Markets have pursued their massive U-turn after what could have been a disastrous geopolitical escalation in the Middle East, which turned out to just be a gigantic TACO.

With the official announcement of a two-week ceasefire, the war premium that heavily discounted stocks over the last month has suddenly vanished.

Market flows have completely reversed, erasing close to 50% of their war-induced moves across major indexes and the FX space.

Global equities are wiping out 28 days of severe corrections in the span of a few sessions (with the largest advance today), leading to what looks like a year's worth of gains packed into one massive relief rally.

The Nasdaq topped over 3%, dragging its peers higher in a display of pure market ecstasy.

Meanwhile, the US dollar took a massive hit, giving up a large chunk of its safe haven gains as risk appetite exploded and plummeting energy prices weighed heavily on the currency.

While the equity side is partying, the commodity space is tumbling. After a gigantic 20% tumble right on the news, WTI crude oil reached a daily bottom around $92 and has started to slowly creep higher.

Traders are now digesting the fact that this deal, while incredibly welcome, currently only lasts for 14 days.

Smart money is actively looking for further clarity and official guidelines regarding the terms of the US-Iran deal to see if this temporary breather can actually translate into a lasting, long-term peace agreement. Until the Strait of Hormuz is fully clear, oil will remain the ultimate barometer for true de-escalation.

After all, the market has welcomed what seems to be a definite change, so let’s get ready for what's next by looking at the intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The daily market action is remarkable, with gigantic gains across the entire market, with a few victims in the more defensive sectors such as utilities and communications, which remained bid throughout the entire war (as rotations and fundamentals helped their outperformance).

The largest gains are seen in producer manufacturing, with gains spanning from 3% to 10% among the largest names, and tech is, of course, loving the turn towards higher beta.

At the other side of the performance spectrum, Energy stocks are getting battered (not surprising with the latest 17% drop in WTI).

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow Jones has offered a remarkable run, indeed exploding above the 47,000 level as previewed in our past day's mid-session review, but is now stalling at the 48,000 Pivotal Resistance.

Any break above 48,000 would put back bulls in long-term advantage; however, they will first have to breach back above the morning highs.

With the current 4H doji, traders are clearly looking for further clarity before moving their pieces further – look for breakouts above (48,088) or below (47,620) for momentum plays.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Pivotal Resistance at 48,000
  • Mini-resistance 48,300
  • Mini-resistance 2 48,700
  • Major Resistance – 49,000 to 49,200

Support Levels

  • Major Pivot 47,400 to 47,600
  • War Resistance now Key Support 47,000 +/- 100 Points (Bearish below)
  • March 8 War lows Resistance now Support 46,300
  • 45,700 to 45,900 August Support
  • January 2025 Highs 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

A similar move has naturally occurred in the Nasdaq, easily extending back towards the 25,000 resistance but is now facing a significant test.

Bulls will have to breach the major psychological resistance in order to mark their higher potential for continuation towards their all-time highs.

  • Reversing from there would point back towards 24,460 (4H 200-MA).

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Key Resistance 25,000 to 25,250
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb Range resistance
  • Major resistance 25,700 to 25,850

Support Levels

  • 24,450 to 24,550 Pivot
  • Feb Range Support 24,150 to 24,200
  • Major 2026 Pivotal Support 23,800 to 24,000
  • August 2025 Support 23,500 to 23,650
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 has exploded to levels not seen since March 11, and now testing a pivotal resistance level, similarly as other Indexes.

Failing to break the morning peak (6,815) would point to a retest of the 4H 200-period MA around 6,700, similarly as the setup seen in Nasdaq.

Breaking 6,690 to the downside, however, would point to 6,550 (which would require further analysis).

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Pivotal Resistance 6,770 to 6,800

  • Key Resistance Zone 6,880 to 6,900

  • 4H 50-period MA 6,550
  • 6,490 to 6,520 October lows
  • 6,300 psychological level (War lows)

data.title: Intercontinental Exchange: Heading For A Challenge Of The High (Upgrade)
data.description:
It has been a while since I wrote about the Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (ICE) on Seeking Alpha. In a May 14, 2024, article highlighting a challenge to the two leading exchanges, valuation was cited as a reason for caution. ICE shares were trading at $133.48 on May 14, 2024, after reaching a record high of $140.43 on March 7, 2024. The CME Group (CME) traded at $207.82 after reaching a record high of $256.94 in early 2022. ICE and CME shares have continued to reach higher lows and higher highs over the past two years. CME rose to $329.16 in March 2026, 28.1% above the early 2022 high. ICE shares reached $189.35 per share in August 2025, 34.8% above the March 2024 high.

ICE’s market cap trails CME’s by over $17 billion on April 9, but ICE could be poised to catch up with its competitor as the energy markets are in focus in 2026.

A bullish trend in ICE shares is approaching a challenge of the August 2025 high

ICE shares reached a record high of $189.35 in August 2025.

The long-term monthly chart shows that after a 24.4% correction to a low of $143.17 in November 2025, ICE shares recovered to $167.66 on April 8. Meanwhile, the long-term trend since ICE shares began trading in November 2005 remains bullish as it has made higher lows and higher highs. If the long-term trend remains intact, it may not be long before ICE shares challenge and climb above the August 2025 all-time high.

Record total average daily volume- ICE and energy markets

According to an April 6, 2026, Seeking Alpha news article, ICE

“sees a 45% year-over-year rise in the Q1 average daily volume, 32% in energy ADV (average daily volume), 29% in agriculture and metals ADV, 65% in financials ADV, 39% in NYSE cash equities ADV, and 23% in NYSE equity options ADV.”

While ICE derives revenue from other sources, such as data, trading volume is the exchange’s bread-and-butter revenue source. Therefore, rising volume goes directly to ICE’s bottom line.

Market volatility stemming from the hostilities in the Middle East has directly affected energy and agricultural trading volumes. The issues surrounding the Strait of Hormuz and other Middle Eastern Sea passages have lifted crude oil and oil product prices. Fertilizer supplies have also been affected, leading to rising agricultural commodity prices and volumes. The prospects for higher inflationary pressures have increased volatility and trading volumes in metals and equity markets.

ICE’s company profile states:

While ICE’s headquarters are in the U.S. in Atlanta, Georgia, the exchange is a leader in trading and clearing of an array of derivative contracts and financial securities across a wide range of asset classes. The bottom line is that increased trading volumes translated to higher revenues and profits for one of the world’s leading trading platforms.

Q4 2025 earnings beat consensus estimates - Q1 2026 earnings should be stellar

ICE reported its latest quarterly earnings on February 5, 2026.

The chart highlights that ICE beat consensus estimates for EPS and revenues. Due to volatile conditions in the Middle East, which are driving higher market volatility, the volume growth in Q1 2026 should lead to even more attractive Q1 results, perhaps exceeding current consensus estimates. On April 6, 2026, ICE reported that it expects higher expenses, but that the incremental expenses will be “more than offset by the related revenues.”

When ICE reports its next earnings on April 30, 2026, the odds favor another EPS and revenue beat.

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades- Valuation is rich

ICE’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grades are a mixed bag in early April 2026.

On May 14, 2024, ICE’s Seeking Alpha Factor Grade in the growth category was a B-, and it has declined slightly to a C in early April 2026. Profitability also slightly declined from an A+ to an A grade. Momentum and revision grades were steady over the period at a C and a B, respectively. Meanwhile, valuation remains ICE’s challenge as the category remains at a failing F grade.

ICE’s P/E ratios, price/book, price/sales, and price/cash flow grades continue to weigh on the exchange’s valuation.

CME is in the same valuation boat - Here is the difference that could cause ICE’s market cap to catch up

ICE’s chief competitor is the CME Group. CME’s current Seeking Alpha Factor Grades are better than ICE’s.

Seeking Alpha’s Factor grades for the CME are the highest possible A+ in the growth category, while profitability and momentum receive A- grades, with an A in the revisions category. CME faces the same problem as ICE: it fails in the valuation category due to its high P/E ratio and price/book, price/sales, and price/cashflow metrics.

The CME’s strength is that it is the world’s most diverse derivatives marketplace and a global hub for managing risk through the trading of futures and options across every major asset class.

ICE’s strength is that it owns the New York Stock Exchange. However, ICE dominates energy and many other commodities futures markets. When it comes to energy, ICE operates the Brent futures contract, the global benchmark for two-thirds of the world’s physical crude oil. The Brent crude oil contract reflects petroleum in Europe, Africa, Russia, and the Middle East. The current geopolitical environment supports higher volumes for ICE’s energy products.

I expect ICE to close the current over $17 billion market-cap gap with the CME, as revenues and profitability likely soared in Q1 2026 and into Q2 amid escalating tensions in the Middle East. ICE and the CME continue to struggle in the valuation category, but ICE’s non-GAAP P/E ratio at just below 24 is lower than CME’s at over 27.50. Therefore, closing the market cap gap could lift ICE shares to new highs over the coming months. Despite the valuation metrics, I rate ICE a Buy in the current environment.

data.title: Stewart Information Services: Q1 Earnings Will Say A Lot About It And The Home-Buying Market
data.description:
With a history that dates back over 125 years, Stewart Information Services Corporation (STC) fills a niche role in the financial sector. The company specializes in title insurance and other real estate services. Stewart assists home buyers and sellers with escrow accounts, settlement services, and post-closing support. The company needs home sales to generate new business, and as the graph of existing US home sales below shows, the last several years have not exactly been great ones to be dependent on US home-buying activity. As 30-year mortgage rates began to climb in 2022, the number of people that were able to afford an average-priced home fell dramatically.

STC was able to piece together a decent performance in 2024 with a total return of about 20%. Last year netted investors about half of that amount. In the first three months of 2026, the stock lost over 13% of its value. The concerns for this year are both internal and market driven. The company spooked investors with a share offering in December that diluted existing shareholders while concerns about profit growth are rising. Mortgage rates have also moved higher in recent months, dampening expectations for a return to a more normal home buying environment.

Last year, I wrote a number of articles about companies that were involved in a related niche industry, private mortgage insurance. Stewart Information Services reminds me of some of those companies like Enact Holdings (ACT) and MGIC Investment Corporation (MTG). They are dependent on home sales and home refinancing to generate business.

The biggest difference is that ACT and MTG have been aggressively buying back their stock to support the share price. STC has been moving in the opposite direction with more of its shares in the market. This is just one of a few reasons that I believe the company is only worth a Hold at the current time. However, I think that the Q1 2026 earnings report from Stewart Information Services could tell us a lot about what the full year will look like for both the company itself and others that depend on home sales to generate new business.

Company Overview

Stewart Information Services operates under the names of over 100 different subsidiaries that are all involved in some aspect of real estate services. Based in Texas, the company does have exposure to international markets in addition to those in the US. STC refers to itself as “the largest global title insurance companies and underwriters in the industry”. Most of the company’s business does take place in the US, however. In Q4 2025, for example, only $38.5 million of the $334.8 million that Stewart generated in title revenue originated in international markets.

The company has a market cap of $1.85 billion and controlled $3.25 billion in total assets at the end of last year. Revenues for the last three years is shown below. Title insurance produced 84.7% of STC’s operating revenues in FY 2025. The company has been able to diversity its revenue mix slightly over the last three years. In FY 2023, title insurance was the source of 88.1% of operating revenues.

In November of last year, Stewart announced its plan to acquire Mortgage Contracting Services. The $330 million deal further diversifies STC away from title insurance policies as Mortgage Contracting Services is in the property preservation industry. This line of business includes activities like property inspections, home repairs for defaulted mortgages, and winterization.

On April 2, a subsidiary of STC announced its plans to acquire Nationwide Appraisal Network, LLC. This will expand the company’s presence in the property appraisal industry. Financial terms of this deal were not disclosed. However, this most recent merger activity for the company will likely increase the allocation for goodwill on the company’s balance sheet which stood at $1.27 billion at the end of last year.

Stewart Information Services offers an attractive dividend yield of 3.47%. Its current quarterly distribution is $0.525 per share, an amount that was increased from $0.50 per share in Q3 2025. The company is currently on a five-year streak of at least one dividend increase per year dating back to the start of 2021 when its payout was only $0.33 per share.

Analysis of Stewart Information Services

As part of its Q4 and FY 2025 earnings conference call back in February, management highlighted improvements in the company’s revenues, earnings, and margins. Domestic commercial revenues increased by 34% year-over-year. Almost all of the improvements made throughout 2025 were organic, as the impact of the Mortgage Contracting Services addition will not be seen until Q1 2026. Adjusted pre-tax margins for STC rose from 5.8% in FY 2024 to 6.8%.

The most impressive part of Stewart’s performance last year was its improvement in net income, even as the housing market was on a bumpy path. In Q4 2025, STC reported net income of $40.9 million, or $1.25 per diluted share, which compared favorably to the $27.2 million, or $0.80 per share, reported in Q4 2024. For the full year, the company had a net income of $130.2 million vs. just $88.2 million in the previous 12-month period. That equated to FY EPS of $4.05, $1.44 more than FY 2024.

Yet even with this performance, the valuation for the stock, even with the decline seen in the first quarter of this year, is still not in what I would call bargain territory. STC trades with a forward PE above 10 and a price to book valuation of 1.12. While those may sound like solid value metrics, this part of the financial sector often trades with single-digit PE ratios and right around book value. Stewart traded at a valuation of eight times earnings or less for most of the time from the second half of 2019 through the end of 2022.

One area to watch in both Q1 and Q2 2026 is the amount Stewart claims for title loss expenses. This number increased 11.2% to $23.0 million in Q4 2025 when compared to Q3. This was a reversal of a trend where these losses had actually decreased in the first nine months of the year. The good news is that operating revenue increased faster than loss claims, and STC’s ratio for losses is lower now than it was in previous years.

Peer Comparison

For further support of my thesis, I compared the picture for STC to two of its peer competitors in the title insurance and real estate services space - First American Financial (FAF) and Fidelity National Financial (FNF). Stewart is clearly the smallest of this bunch, which would normally mean that its two competitors here would have the advantage of benefitting from greater economies of scale.

The most recent financial data presents a mixed picture. STC trades at a higher valuation but also shows the potential for greater profit growth, at least for the year ahead. Whether it is their size or other internal operating efficiencies, FAF and FNF are able to greater margins. Both FAF and FNF also have a higher dividend yield, providing some cushion when the markets head south.

A look at the total return for this trio shows that none of them have really enjoyed the last 12 months. FAF investors may be the least upset by their losses. STC’s decline of 14.1%, when dividends are included, is still better than the 26.1% lost by FNF. It should be noted that FNF does lead this group when returns are stretched out to longer periods of time. Fidelity National Financial has produced returns of 43.9% and 187.8% over the last five and ten years, respectively. That easily tops STC’s returns of 31.5% and 139.8% during the same time periods.

Outlook for Q1 2026 Earnings

The first quarter of the year is usually the weakest for the company from an earnings perspective, as home-buying activity slows considerably in the winter months. April, May, and June are "peak buying season" according to the National Association of Realtors, and home sales are weakest in December, January, and February. This gives buyers more leverage to negotiate pricing, which can lead to lower sales prices.

This home buying activity pattern definitely shows up in Stewart's bottom line. In FY 2025, STC reported earnings of $4.63 in Q2 through Q4 combined. In Q1, EPS totaled only $0.25. In FY 2024, the company reported a profit of $3.20 in the final three quarters of the year, but only $0.17 in Q1. In Q1 2023, the company actually reported a loss of $0.25 per share due to what the company called "historically low transaction volumes".

Yet, analysts are expecting adjusted earnings of $0.53 per share for Q1 2026, which would be a 112% improvement from last year. Existing home sales in FY 2026 were only expected to rise about 1.7%, according to Realtor.com. After buyers were teased with lower rates to start the year, 30-year mortgages are now priced at their highest level since September of last year. US bond yields have been rising, in part because of the recent conflict in the Middle East.

The average 30-year rate climbed above 6.5% last month. In a recent article that appeared on CNBC, Jake Krimmel, senior economist at Realtor.com, was quoted as saying, “As the housing market approaches the ‘best time to sell’ season, it sits in a precarious position, caught between long-term improvements and sudden short-term instability. Everything seems much more unsettled and uncertain than it did just a month ago.” New listings are down, but active inventory is climbing because homes are sitting on the market longer.

If rates had stayed around 6%, as they were in mid-January, I would be more optimistic about STC meeting, or even exceeding, its projected EPS of $0.53, but the last five or six weeks appear to have made that target more challenging. Fewer home sales and refinancing opportunities mean fewer policies for title insurance. It also means fewer opportunities for the other real estate services that the company offers.

The Bottom Line

Barring a deeper collapse in the real estate market or a significant push higher for mortgage rates, I believe Q2 2026 is likely to be better for Stewart Information Services than Q1 has usually been, even when adjusting for the seasonality of home sales in the US and abroad. The valuation of STC is close to where I might consider a soft buy recommendation, but the current time appears to be too soon for that action now. If Q1 numbers for the company do come in stronger than expected, I think that could be a good sign for the company in the remainder of this year. However, my own pessimism for Q1 2026 keeps me on the sidelines for a company that is worth watching whenever home buying activity does begin to normalize.

data.title: BlackRock Credit Relative Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 0.61% (Institutional shares)(BGCIX) and 0.48% (Investor A shares, without sales charge)(BGCAX) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • On a regional basis, the United States was the main contributor to performance, though European exposure was meaningfully accretive. The carry strategies performed best, while the traditional and absolute return strategies were also meaningful contributors. The hedging strategies detracted from returns.
  • During the quarter, the fund's gross exposure was lowered from 148% to 123%. From a net exposure perspective, the fund's net long exposure increased from 70% to 81%, while there was a small net gain in cash bonds. The allocation to bank loans was reduced.

Contributors

On a regional basis, the United States led performance, though Europe was also a strong contributor. In the United States, the absolute strategies were the top performer, led by event and thematic trades around media. The U.S. traditional strategies contributed, particularly front-end investment grade and short duration (low interest rate sensitivity) high yield allocations, along with the U.S. carry strategies, where artificial intelligence-related trades and enhanced equipment trust certificates boosted returns. In Europe, the carry strategies drove performance, led by collateralized loan obligations and bank loans, while the traditional strategies, particularly high yield and financials exposures, were also beneficial. The European absolute strategies, in aggregate, detracted, though select event-related technology and packaging names contributed.

Detractors

During the quarter, the detractors were limited and were led by the hedging strategies in both the United States and Europe. Select opportunistic and idiosyncratic short exposures around hospitality and workplace technology also weighed on returns.

Further insight

Looking at 2026, while we are optimistic overall about the growth scenario and lean more long in the fund, we are cognizant of the tightness in spreads and prevalence of dispersion within asset classes and sectors. As such, we are focused on bottom-up opportunities to boost performance and continue to prefer to capitalize on these rather than own expressions of more generic beta (market sensitivity). We expect the absolute strategies to drive the majority of opportunities in the medium term, and take a slightly higher-quality, higher-liquidity tilt in the traditional and carry strategies.

data.title: Zscaler: A Generational Opportunity To 'Buy' AI Tailwinds At A Discount
data.description:
The stock market's backlash against software stocks in 2026 has been one of the most surprising shifts in sentiment in recent memory. By and large, the selling pressure has been indiscriminate, with investors dumping any stocks that have the slightest whiff of potentially being disrupted by AI.

Cybersecurity software stocks, in particular, have been hard hit ever since Anthropic's Claude released more robust security features. Zscaler (ZS), which made a name for itself as a cloud-first security platform, has been especially hard hit, with its stock down nearly 40% since the start of the year. The question for investors now is, will the pain continue, or is this just a temporary slump?

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Zscaler in December, when the stock was trading at $240 per share. Since then, shares of Zscaler have nearly been cut in half. Needless to say, I acknowledge that my "Buy" rating call was ill-timed, and I certainly didn't predict the SaaSpocalypse that would wipe billions in market value off the table. That said, I also see zero red flags in this company's trajectory and am happy to take the opportunity to dollar-cost average down. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

It's impossible to discuss Zscaler or any cybersecurity stock in early 2026 without mentioning the threat (or lack thereof) from AI. Broadly speaking, I see AI as a net tailwind for the company, without dismissing the threat from new security software companies that can spring up and displace existing incumbents.

The chart below showcases the principles of Zscaler's "zero trust" architecture. The company's core differentiation versus standard firewalls is that Zscaler manages each connection individually (for example, between a user's endpoint device and its request for data from a particular cloud application), assuming "zero trust" in that exchange.

AI is just an extension of this platform. In fact, the rise of agentic AI means a substantial increase in the number of endpoints by which data leakage could occur. Zscaler's own revenue growth and RPO expansion suggest that AI is a net growth opportunity for the company, not a risk.

The other general fear that is attacking software stocks is the risk that mass layoffs driven by AI may have on seat-based software companies. This favors companies that price their products on a consumption basis, rather than by headcount. Zscaler already checks off this box. As shown below, its core pricing is based on traffic and workloads, which will only increase as agentic AI is added into the mix

Of course, we should touch as well on AI-based competition. It's possible that the foundational LLMs like OpenAI and Anthropic release more robust AI products; or that startups leveraging those models could build new cybersecurity tools. But in my view, few companies (especially large organizations) will be willing to rip out their mission-critical security architecture in favor of an untested startup. We note as well that Claude's recent source code leak doesn't exactly inspire much confidence in Anthropic's security capabilities. In short, we don't dismiss the possibility of AI competition - but we also shouldn't claim that competition will wipe out Zscaler and incumbent cybersecurity companies either, which is what current market moves imply.

Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Zscaler's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q2 (January quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Zscaler's revenue grew 26% y/y to $815.8 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $799.0 million (+23% y/y) by a three-point margin. To me, Zscaler's latest results contain plenty of evidence that the company isn't under any threat from AI but rather is benefiting from sales of new AI-driven products and workloads.

We note that revenue growth didn't decelerate at all from 26% y/y growth in Q1, despite an arguably tougher macroeconomic backdrop. Also, Zscaler continues to substantially grow its RPO (remaining performance obligations, or essentially backlog). Backlog growth outpaced revenue growth, up 31% y/y to a staggering $6.1 billion. We note that Zscaler has only guided to ~$3.3 billion revenue for FY26. This indicates that Zscaler has roughly two years' worth of revenue in contractual backlog.

Customers who were thinking of jumping ship from Zscaler wouldn't be placing long-term backlogs with the company, which is the core indication that IT companies aren't looking to displace Zscaler with AI-driven solutions anytime soon. Key to the company's success in landing new bookings is the relatively new Z-Flex program, which allows customers to make multi-year spending commitments without identifying which products and modules they want to spend it on. In effect, Zscaler is now allowing customers to mix and match their spending, giving CIOs plenty of forward flexibility. The company notes that its Z-Flex program has helped to upsell customers into larger deals while shortening sales cycles amid a tough macro. As shown below, bookings made via Z-Flex increased 65% sequentially to $290 million in Q2:

The company's CFO, Kevin Rubin, noted as well that sales productivity has improved and that the company is entering into calendar 2026 with a healthy, high-conversion pipeline. Per his remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

We are pleased with the strong execution in our account-centric sales motion, which is strengthening our position as a long-term strategic partner and driving deeper customer adoption over time. In Q2, we again delivered double-digit sales productivity growth, reflecting continued improvement in our go-to-market execution with meaningful headroom ahead. We also achieved record pipeline conversion for Q2, signaling stronger pipeline quality and improved visibility.

We continued to build strong momentum this quarter with our recently launched Z-Flex program. Z-Flex gives customers with multiyear commitments, the flexibility to activate or swap modules without starting a new procurement cycle, along with premium deployment assistance and support. This program is driving meaningful upsell, shorter sales cycles and greater forward visibility.

Zscaler isn't ignoring profitability either. As shown below, pro forma operating margins held flat y/y at 22%:

On top of 26% revenue growth, Zscaler firmly remains a "Rule of 40" software company.

Valuation and key takeaways

The biggest appeal to buying Zscaler now is that the stock is trading at fire-sale multiples. At current share prices near $138, Zscaler trades at a market cap of $22.87 billion. Netting off the $3.51 billion of cash and $1.70 billion of convertible debt on Zscaler's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $21.06 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (which ends in July 2026), Zscaler is guiding to $3.309-$3.322 billion in revenue, or ~24% y/y growth. Its FCF guidance margin midpoint implies ~$888 million in FCF.

For next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting the company to generate $3.97 billion in revenue (+20% y/y). If we assume ~flat FCF margins at 27% in FY27, FCF would be $1.07 billion. This positions Zscaler's valuation multiples at:

  • 6.4x EV/FY26 revenue and 5.3x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 23.7x EV/FY26 FCF and 19.7x EV/FY27 FCF

To me, this is a rare opportunity to buy a "Rule of 40" company at a substantial discount. In my view, once we move through several more quarters in which Zscaler showcases that AI is not a headwind to its growth rates, the company will be well-positioned for an upward rerating.

data.title: Palantir: The World Changed When Silicon Valley Met Defense (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), which is up since my last analysis by +15%, where I issued a Hold rating, has now found somewhat of a base in price. Barring macro considerations like Hormuz causing compression across the equity board (now easing), I think this could be quite a good entry point. My thesis here is based more on fundamentals and long-term growth rather than analyzing price and entry points. I begin the thesis with an obvious caveat for you to keep in mind: Palantir is expensive, but that does not mean it won't continue to rise in price.

Palantir Is NATO's Core Modernization Engine

World military expenditure rose 9.4% in 2024, which is the steepest annual increase since at least 1988. Whether you like it or not, that puts Palantir in a compounding vector rather than a short-term cyclical shift. However, investors in PLTR stock need to be aware that its valuation can still compress even in a structural military expansion environment. The IMF has recorded that major geopolitical shocks have cut aggregate stock prices by 3% on average and as much as 9% in some instances; the ECB also says that such shocks tighten financial conditions and raise risk premia.

Palantir's ontology matters economically because it maps data into context and links data, logic, and actions inside a decision-centric architecture. Inevitably, this leads to faster operations in organizations that have data abundance and coordination scarcity.

The alliance angle is one that is strong for Palantir and also potentially undertapped. NATO itself has warned that weak modernization raises interoperability gaps, and Palantir also reported about $4.4 billion of government remaining deal value from the United States and allied countries, so part of the moat sits in coalition architecture.

However, one core risk for Palantir right now is if NATO breaks up. Of course, we know from countless news headlines that the Trump administration is considering it, and this is negative for Palantir, as it really could shine long-term in alliance interoperability. I think a U.S. withdrawal from NATO could dent the PLTR stock price, but there are also significant hurdles to get through for such an event to be a reality. One would hope, if a PLTR shareholder, that NATO can be reformed and directed properly so that America feels comfortable again being a part of and leading it.

In terms of commercial revenue, the center of gravity is shifting this way as management is guiding more than $3.144 billion of U.S. commercial revenue in 2026, up at least 115%. The next phase could be fueled by large firms rewriting workflows around AI, with Palantir's ontology front and center.

But the most exciting aspect of Palantir is still its Silicon Valley roots, with founder Thiel and a small gang of revolutionary upstarts bringing world-changing technology to the forefront of the defense industry of the West. DoD says that programs using the Software Acquisition Pathway grew at roughly a 50% annual rate in FY23-24 while also moving to establish a new software cadre, so Silicon Valley methods are literally being absorbed into defense institutions.

Valuation and Price Analysis

Based on a technical perspective, Palantir is cheaper than it seems when looking at raw fundamentals. In fact, traditional fundamentalists won't touch PLTR, and that's fine. Leave it to the visionaries, the technical analysts, and even the speculators. Those folks often make more money than traditionalists anyway, even if it's rarer to find the good ones and they operate more dangerously.

As you can see in the chart above, the weekly RSI is just under 50 as I write this, indicating relatively fair sentiment. Moreover, the stock price is now around the 50-week MA. I admit, there is no value here. There is just not excessive sentiment like what we saw throughout 2025. That said, those who bought in early 2025 when the price was far above the 50-week MA made a killing, so these trendlines shouldn't be relied on in isolation.

Nonetheless, without going into a quagmire of technical indicators, which I respect that some analysts specialize in (I don't), buyers of PLTR stock are essentially making a concentrated bet on whether market sentiment can be sustained by brand value and forward fundamental growth expectations, despite a forward PEG 85% above the sector median. I think it's plausible that its sentiment will be sustained, but as I have mentioned before, there is de facto no margin of safety here.

If April 2028 normalized EPS comes in at $3, and the forward P/E is about 85 in April 2027 (down from the present of over 100), then the stock price will be about $255 in 12 months. The implied upside is 70%. This is my bull-case outcome.

Alternatively, if the forward PEG reverts toward the sector median, we may see the stock price at ~$200 or lower. This is my base-case outcome, reflecting valuation gravity realities even though there is a significant likelihood that the market stays "irrational" about Palantir simply because the company is so unique, so strong, so mythologized, and equally remarkably high-growth.

Risks

The obvious main risk for PLTR stock, which I will repeat here again, is multiple compression.

Beyond that, we also have Hormuz. While this risk is now easing following the two-week ceasefire, which I am quite confident will evolve into a long-term peace, further disruption here could obviously cause multiple compressions across the equity board and deflate PLTR.

It's also important to understand that many of Palantir's contracts with U.S. and allied countries (it does not deal only with NATO) can be terminated for convenience. Furthermore, U.S. options cannot be exercised more than one year out. These factors create volatility risk.

Finally, Europe is rearming, but it also wants more industrial autonomy. The EU's Readiness 2030/EDIP agenda is designed to strengthen and modernize Europe's own defense industrial base, which could mean it seeks other vendors to Palantir where possible. Though Palantir is so elite and specialized, this may often be difficult. Palantir also says export controls and sanctions can block non-U.S. deployments, while privacy laws and AI rules can raise compliance costs via complexity (notable in Europe).

Conclusion: Buy

Palantir has changed the world, but it also has an immense responsibility as a result. It is no longer a gang of upstarts looking to revolutionize the modernity of the free world's defense ecosystem. Instead, it is a leader, and leaders are judged not only by competence but also by values and strategy. There is the potential for Palantir to rise into the Apple (AAPL) of defense; indeed, it is very conceivable, but it requires patience, humility, and a steady hand from management that does not view war as a profit vector but rather peace as the long-term compounding horizon instead. I'm bullish, targeting 30-40% upside in 12 months in the base case.

data.title: CURE: Pullback In Healthcare Stocks Presents A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

U.S. healthcare stocks have marginally underperformed the S&P 500 so far in 2026, largely driven by profit taking after a strong performance in late 2025. Amid energy price volatility which naturally increases uncertainty in the U.S. and global economic outlooks, I think this pullback presents a buying opportunity, as the sector trades at attractive forward valuations and will likely see a limited profitability hit if we do see some of the downside economic risks materialize.

Investors looking to make a leveraged bet on a rebound in U.S. healthcare stocks may want to consider the Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3X ETF (CURE). The bullish investment thesis for CURE can be summarized as:

  • Constituents of CURE's underlying index trade at a circa 17% forward P/E discount relative to the S&P 500, quite attractive in light of healthcare's non-cyclical nature.
  • Analysts envisage double-digit gains of about 17.14% for CURE's ten largest holdings over the next 12 months, notably better than my prior coverage in November 2025.
  • CURE will benefit from robust earnings growth post 2027 thanks to an ageing U.S. population and AI implementation in drug development.

In my prior CURE article I was indeed advocating for some profit taking and/or switching to the unlevereged XLV ETF, which proved pertinent. The Buy rating however turned out to be overly bullish, as CURE has notably underperformed the S&P 500 amid a broad risk-off mood in U.S. equity markets. Fast forward to April 2026, I see the setup for CURE as much more constructive, with more opportunistic investors potentially having the opportunity to switch from XLV to CURE if they are comfortable with the increased volatility.

ETF Strategy and Characteristics

CURE aims to deliver a return that is 300% (3x) the daily change in the benchmark Health Care Select Sector Index. As such, if the underlying index rises by 1%, CURE should deliver a daily gain of about 3%. The reverse is also valid — a 1% loss in the underlying index would lead to a 3% daily decline in CURE.

Since CURE only aims to replicate daily performance, its long-term performance may be affected by factors such as beta slippage and the 0.94% expense ratio, as I will discuss in the risks section.

These characteristics make CURE more of a trading tool rather than a buy-and-hold ETF, although the favorable long-term outlook for healthcare stocks arguably makes CURE one of the few leveraged ETFs you could hold for long periods of time.

Near-term Catalysts

In the near term, going long CURE can deliver attractive returns if we see de-escalation in the Iran war, which would be a catalyst for the broader U.S. markets.

The ETF may also provide a trading opportunity during earnings season, with many top holdings such as Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) due to report earnings on April 30 and April 14, respectively. Eli Lilly in particular looks particularly attractive, as the stock is down roughly 13% year-to-date, presenting circa 30% upside to 12-month analyst price targets.

Against this backdrop, I think the bar for a share price increase in LLY is not particularly high. With a 13.95% weight in CURE's underlying index, any material gains in LLY will also translate into sizable gains for CURE.

Long-Term Opportunity

While holding CURE for extended periods of time may raise a few eyebrows, if you look at the ETF's 10-year total return versus the unlevereged XLV ETF (which tracks the same underlying index), you will see that CURE has still managed to outperform, albeit outperformance was largely driven by the long bull market of 2016-2021, with XLV gradually catching up in 2022-2026:

If you study CURE's relative performance, you will see that buying the ETF after a downturn in XLV has been quite rewarding. In essence, while holding CURE during a correction in XLV can eat a lot of your gains, a subsequent recovery can allow for a favorable risk-reward trading opportunity.

With XLV down in the mid-single digits so far in 2026, I think the setup for a long-term trade in CURE is quite good (assuming we get a rebound in healthcare stocks).

The other principal factor to consider is that the U.S. population is aging, with the share of the 65 or older population projected to keep increasing in the coming decades:

This population dynamic creates a long-term catalyst for healthcare ETFs such as CURE, as the number of patients requiring treatment will only increase.

Last but not least, the adoption of AI in clinical research should allow for a faster development of new molecules and treatments, potentially increasing margins at healthcare companies while providing new treatment options for patients. This again is a potential catalyst for CURE over the long term.

When To Buy and Sell

Now that we have outlined some near and long term catalysts, I can share my personal experience with 300% leveraged ETFs. Firstly, only invest amounts you are willing to see significant daily valuation changes on, as these ETFs can be quite volatile. For me personally, that is a very small amount of the portfolio which however could ultimately contribute to overall returns.

As a rule of thumb, I like to buy 3x long ETFs during periods of significant drawdowns in the underlying index. Hence keeping a close eye on the unlevereged XLV ETF is arguably essential. You can use an approach of adding a CURE position for every 5% or 10% drawdowns in XLV, potentially taking profits on CURE when XLV recovers and subsequently manages to post sizable gains in a short period of time.

Indeed since CURE is a daily leveraged ETF, you would have to monitor your position on an almost daily, taking advantage of price spikes to take profits. While this is not the only approach you can use with CURE, it has worked for me well.

Current Valuation

Turning to valuations, I calculate that CURE's holdings trade at 17.59x their forward earnings, representing a circa 17% discount relative to the 21.06x forward P/E for the S&P 500. I think this is quite attractive as health care is a defensive sector, benefiting from long-term tailwinds such as an ageing U.S. population and an increased focus on well-being.

What is more, increased adoption of AI may streamline and optimize research processes, potentially improving margins at healthcare companies. As such, despite its non-cyclical nature, I think healthcare stocks will deliver robust earnings growth post 2026 as well. This upside is also reflected in 12-month price targets by Wall Street analysts, which I will discuss in the next section.

Analyst Price Targets

CURE's underlying index is quite concentrated, with the ten largest holdings accounting for almost 60% of the index. While this makes CURE returns dependent on the performance of just a few companies, it also allows us to quickly gauge the 12-month upside potential for the ETF before the effects of leverage.

In the table below, I have compiled the weights and upside potential to 12-month price targets for CURE's ten largest holdings:

Company Weight in Underlying Index 12-month Upside Potential
Eli Lilly and Compant (LLY) 13.95% 30.43%
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 11.02% 0.12%
AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV) 6.94% 20.54%
Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) 5.64% 6.71%
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) 4.72% 27.17%
Amgen Inc. (AMGN) 3.52% 2.29%
Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) 3.47% 34.87%
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) 3.36% 29.15%
Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) 3.26% 12.35%
Pfizer Inc. (PFE) 3.03% 2.69%
Total 58.91% 17.14%

Source: Author calculations

Looking at the table above, we see that CURE's ten largest holdings offer upside of about 17.14% over the next 12 months, which is quite attractive for a defensive ETF selection. I arrive at the approximate 17.14% contribution by using the weights of the ten largest CURE positions (58.91% in total) to create a portfolio where 100% of assets are invested (this corrects for the remaining 41.09% of assets which are invested across dozens of stocks but analyst price targets are not easily available). This new portfolio is then weighed by analyst price targets to arrive at a contribution to CURE, as shown in the snippet below. While it is not a perfect approach, I think it is efficient for ETFs such as CURE which exhibit high concentration.

What is more, analyst price targets imply that not a single top ten holding should be trading lower in twelve months' time, which is quite a bullish backdrop.

Overall, we see a notable change relative to analyst expectations from November 2025, as discussed in my previous article somewhat appropriately titled "Analysts Strike a Cautious Tone Heading Into 2026". Back then, analyst price targets only reflected a circa 0.77% gain over the next 12 months for CURE's ten largest holdings.

To conclude, the recent correction in healthcare stocks, coupled with analyst price target revisions, has resulted in a much more constructive outlook for CURE's top ten holdings over the next year.

CURE Rating

With attractive valuations and sizable upside over the 12 months, the setup for a leveraged healthcare trade with CURE appears quite favorable. While a more cautious outlook may be warranted if we look at current geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, I think their effect on healthcare companies should be quite limited over the medium to long term.

As such, I still believe that a Buy rating is warranted for CURE. If anything, the improved outlook relative to my prior article from November 2025 calls for a rotation into CURE from the unlevereged State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV), which tracks the same underlying index. Of course, CURE's potentially higher gains come with notable risks, which I will discuss in the next section.

Risks

As with any 300% leveraged ETF, significant near-term downside losses are a key risk in the investment case. Most importantly, these may materialize against an otherwise bullish setup for healthcare stocks. For instance, during the COVID-19 2020 market crash, CURE lost over 60% of its value in a single month despite the pandemic ultimately being a catalyst for healthcare earnings.

As such, while the current outlook for CURE appears quite favorable, the ETF may still deliver losses if stocks in general come under pressure. In the near term, further escalation in the Middle East war is a clear risk in this regard.

Beta slippage (the decline in net asset value leveraged ETFs experience if prices move up and down) is another risk specific to CURE. This may result in CURE underperforming the unlevereged XLF ETF in a sideways market.

Indeed investors who cannot bear the volatility associated with CURE's daily movements but still want to capture the upside offered by health care stocks should consider the unlevereged XLV ETF. You can read more about the risk-return profile and risks associated with leveraged ETFs on the SEC website here.

Last but not least, regulatory risk remains a concern for healthcare stocks, with President Trump's focus on reducing drug prices for consumers potentially resulting in a margin hit for healthcare companies. In this respect, analyst EPS estimates and 12-month price targets may prove to be overly optimistic.

Conclusion

U.S. healthcare stocks are off to a weak start in 2026 as investors take profits after a strong performance in late 2025. I believe this has resulted in a buying opportunity in the sector, with CURE holdings trading at a circa 17% forward P/E discount relative to the S&P 500.

The attractive valuation is also reflected in analyst price targets, which envisage an approximately 17.14% gain for CURE's ten largest holdings over the next year. As such, notwithstanding risks principally stemming from CURE's high leverage and beta slippage, I confirm my previous Buy rating on CURE.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Short Selling Points To Higher Prices
data.description:
Two indicators made by comparing buying of ProShares short versus long funds point to higher stock prices.

The Theory of Contrary Opinion

The theory of contrary opinion is a theory used by every great investor.

It says when “too many” investors believe stock prices will rise, they usually fall. Likewise, when “too many” expect prices to drop, they tend to rise. The theory is easy to state but hard to apply. The key is knowing when there are “too many” investors expecting the same thing.

Indicators That Measure Investor Expectations

To apply the theory, you cannot guess when “too many” are expecting something; you need indicators to determine it. You also need historical data across many bull and bear markets to discover when an indicator has signaled “too many” in the past. For example, an indicator might show two bulls for every bear, which might seem like “too many” but it may require a ratio closer to four to one.

We use 16 sentiment indicators at the Sentiment King. Some are well known; the two I’m going to show you here are unique to us.

Short Selling Indicators

It's insightful to compare how many investors are short selling stocks versus going long. We think the best way to measure this is through the ProShares fund family. They provide ETF's that are either long and short an index. They also have funds that use leverage.

Their Ultra funds use 2X leverage while their UltraPro funds use 3X. We compare the amount of buying in the long and short ultra funds as well as buying an all the long and short UltraPro funds.

The Ultra 2X Funds

The bottom portion of the chart below is the ratio of the amount of buying in the Ultra 2X short funds to the amount of buying in the Ultra 2X long funds. The black line represents 1.0 where the buying is equal.

You can see from the chart that the curve is usually under 1, which means speculators are going long more than they're going short. The ratio is currently around 1.4. This means they're buying 40% more of the short 2X funds than the long 2X funds.

We've used arrows to point to past times in the last fifteen years when the ratio got near or above 1.4. All but one marked the start of a significant rally.

The UltraPro 3X Funds

This chart is the ratio of the amount of buying in the UltraPro 3X short funds to the amount of buying in the UltraPro 3X long funds. The black line represents 1.0 where the buying is equal.

Buying in the 3X UltraPro funds produces a different pattern than the 2X funds.

In the seven years between 2014 and 2022, buying of the short funds never exceeded that of the long funds. At times the ratio got down to 0.2, which means five times more money was going into long funds than short funds.

The last time buying in short funds exceeded long funds was during the lows of the bear market of 2022. We’ve indicated past periods with arrows. Each was a good buying opportunity.

You can see that the ratio recently rose above 1.0 for the first time since 2022. To us this is a very bullish sign.

Summary

The amount of buying of ProShare 2X and 3X short funds compared to buying in the corresponding long funds is at levels that indicated major buying opportunities over the last 15 years.

We think this is further indication that our forecast of a second advancing wave following last April’s nine month advance is correct.
